Nitai Das

Abstract

The Notification Pro project is a premium Android application designed to automatically capture, organize, and secure all incoming device notifications in real time. In everyday smartphone usage, important notifications from messaging apps, emails, banking alerts, and other services are often dismissed accidentally or lost in a cluttered notification shade, leading users to miss critical information.

This project addresses the problem by running a persistent background service that intercepts every notification posted by installed applications, extracts its content — including title, text, sender icons, and media attachments — and stores it in a structured local SQLite database powered by Android Room. The system is built using Kotlin as the primary programming language and follows the MVVM (Model-View-ViewModel) architectural pattern with the Repository design pattern to ensure clean separation of concerns and maintainability.

Key features include an interactive analytics dashboard with daily, weekly, and monthly notification statistics; a powerful full-text search and filtering engine; smart duplicate detection and noise filtering algorithms that suppress repetitive notifications from VPN apps, media scanners, and download progress bars; multi-layered security with PIN lock, pattern lock, and biometric (fingerprint/face) authentication; PDF export and sharing capabilities; per-app enable/disable controls; and configurable auto-delete policies managed by Android WorkManager.

The application targets Android 7.0 (API 24) and above, supporting devices up to Android 14 (API 35). The user interface is crafted using Material Design 3 with dynamic theming, providing a modern, premium look with support for both Dark and Light modes. Notification Pro eliminates the risk of losing important notifications, enhances user productivity, and provides a secure, organized, and visually appealing notification management experience.

Keywords: Android, Notification Management, Kotlin, MVVM, Room Database, NotificationListenerService, Material Design 3, Biometric Authentication, WorkManager, Paging 3

Chapter 1: Introduction

1.1 Background

Smartphones have become an integral part of modern life, serving as the primary communication channel for billions of users worldwide. Every day, the average smartphone user receives between 60 to 80 notifications from various installed applications, ranging from instant messaging and social media to email, banking, health, and system updates. These notifications serve as the primary mechanism for applications to communicate time-sensitive information to users.

However, the Android operating system treats notifications as inherently transient — once dismissed from the notification shade (either by user action or system clearing), the information is permanently and irrecoverably lost. Android does provide a rudimentary "Notification Log" accessible through a hidden Settings widget on some devices, but it offers only a bare-bones list without search, filtering, media preservation, or organizational capabilities.

This transience creates a significant usability problem. Users frequently miss critical alerts from banking applications, urgent emails, time-sensitive offers, and important personal messages simply because they accidentally swiped away a notification or their device cleared notifications during a period of inattention. The information gap becomes especially pronounced for users who rely heavily on their smartphones for professional and financial activities.

1.2 Problem Statement

In the current Android ecosystem, there is no robust, user-friendly mechanism to persistently capture, organize, search, and secure notification history. Existing third-party solutions on the Google Play Store suffer from critical limitations:

  • No intelligent filtering: Databases become cluttered with repetitive VPN bandwidth updates, download progress notifications, and media scanner spam, making it difficult to find meaningful notifications.
  • No media preservation: Profile pictures, shared images, and app icons from notifications are discarded, losing valuable visual context.
  • Weak or absent security: Sensitive notification data (banking alerts, personal messages) is stored without encryption or biometric protection.
  • Outdated user interfaces: Most existing solutions use Material Design 1 or 2 standards, resulting in a dated and unprofessional appearance.
  • No analytics or organization: Users cannot track notification patterns, browse by date, or manage notifications on a per-app basis.

The Notification Pro project directly addresses these gaps by providing a comprehensive, premium-grade notification management solution.

1.3 Motivation

The motivation for this project stems from both personal experience and academic research. As a smartphone user, the developer frequently encountered situations where important notifications were accidentally dismissed — a banking OTP that expired before it could be read, a meeting reminder that was swiped away during a busy moment, or a critical message from a colleague that was lost in a flood of less important notifications.

Academic research by Mehrotra et al. (2016) and Pielot et al. (2014) further validates this problem at scale, demonstrating that notification overload and accidental dismissal are widespread issues affecting millions of smartphone users globally. These studies establish a strong foundation for the development of intelligent, persistent notification management systems.

1.4 Objectives

The primary objectives of the Notification Pro project are:

  1. To develop a persistent notification capture service using Android's NotificationListenerService that automatically intercepts and stores all incoming notifications without any manual user interaction.
  2. To implement intelligent noise filtering algorithms that suppress repetitive and low-value notifications from VPN apps, media scanners, download progress bars, and grouped message summaries.
  3. To build a smart duplicate detection system using composite keys (package name + title + text) with time-based deduplication thresholds to prevent redundant storage.
  4. To provide a comprehensive analytics dashboard with daily, weekly, and monthly notification statistics, interactive calendar browsing, and most-active-apps ranking.
  5. To implement multi-layered security with PIN lock, pattern lock, and biometric authentication (fingerprint/face) to protect sensitive notification data.
  6. To enable rich media extraction from notifications including app icons, profile avatars, and shared images, preserving full visual context.
  7. To support full-text search and filtering across all captured notifications by app, date range, or saved status.
  8. To provide PDF export and sharing capabilities for creating permanent records of important notifications.
  9. To implement configurable auto-delete policies (global 30-day retention and per-app 24-hour cleanup) managed by Android WorkManager.
  10. To deliver a premium user experience using Material Design 3 with dynamic theming, dark/light mode support, and smooth animations.

1.5 Scope of the Project

The scope of this project encompasses the complete design, development, testing, and documentation of a premium-grade Android application that provides:

  • Notification Interception: Real-time capture of all notifications using NotificationListenerService API with foreground service and boot persistence.
  • Data Management: Structured storage using Room (SQLite) with indexed queries for packageName, timestamp, and isSaved fields.
  • User Interface: A Material Design 3 interface with dashboard analytics, notification browsing, search/filter, saved items, per-app controls, and security settings.
  • Security: Multi-method app lock (PIN, pattern, biometric) with encrypted preferences and configurable lock timeouts.
  • Background Processing: Automated cleanup via WorkManager, boot persistence via BroadcastReceiver, and battery-efficient service management.

1.5.1 Limitations

  • The application requires explicit user permission to access the Notification Listener Service, which must be granted manually through Android Settings.
  • Notification capture is limited to content exposed by the posting application through standard Android notification APIs.
  • Data is stored locally on-device only; cloud backup and cross-device synchronization are not included in the current scope.
  • Battery consumption may vary across different device manufacturers due to aggressive battery optimization policies (e.g., Xiaomi, Huawei).
  • The application targets Android 7.0 (API 24) and above; older versions are not supported.

1.6 Organization of the Report

This report is organized into nine chapters. Chapter 1 provides the introduction, problem statement, and objectives. Chapter 2 presents the literature survey comparing existing and proposed systems. Chapter 3 details the system requirements and feasibility study. Chapter 4 covers system design including architecture, diagrams, and database schema. Chapter 5 discusses the implementation with annotated code snippets. Chapter 6 presents screenshots and UI walkthrough. Chapter 7 documents the testing methodology and results. Chapter 8 provides the conclusion and future scope. Chapter 9 lists all references.

Chapter 2: Literature Survey

2.1 Introduction

This chapter reviews the existing body of knowledge related to mobile notification management, examines current solutions available in the market, and establishes the research foundation that motivated the development of Notification Pro. The survey covers published academic research, existing third-party applications, and the technical landscape of Android notification APIs.

2.2 Published Research

Mehrotra et al. (2016) conducted extensive research on content-driven intelligent notification mechanisms for mobile applications, published in ACM Computing Surveys. Their work demonstrated that users are frequently bombarded with low-value alerts, leading to habituated and accidental dismissals of critical information. The study found that an average smartphone user receives 63.5 notifications per day, with a significant portion being dismissed without being read.

Pielot, Church, and de Oliveira (2014) conducted an in-situ study of mobile phone notifications, published in ACM MobileHCI, which demonstrated the sheer scale of notification loss in daily mobile usage. Their research established a strong academic foundation for the necessity of persistent, automated notification capture systems that can preserve notification history beyond the transient notification shade.

Weber et al. (2018) investigated user-defined deferral of mobile notifications in their work "Snooze!" published in ACM MobileHCI. Their findings revealed that users often want to revisit notifications at a later time but lack the tools to do so effectively, further supporting the need for a persistent notification history system.

2.3 Existing System Analysis

In the existing system, Android users have no built-in mechanism to save or recover dismissed notifications beyond the native notification log. This hidden log provides only a bare-bones list of recent notifications without search, filtering, organization, or media extraction capabilities.

Existing third-party notification history applications on the Google Play Store offer basic logging but typically suffer from the following limitations:

  • They lack intelligent noise filtering, leading to databases cluttered with VPN status updates, download progress bars, and media scanner spam.
  • They do not extract rich media such as profile pictures and shared images from notifications.
  • Security features like biometric app lock are absent or rudimentary.
  • They do not provide analytics dashboards or per-app management controls.
  • Their user interfaces follow outdated design standards, resulting in a dated aesthetic.
  • Most store notification data in plain text without encryption, raising privacy concerns.

2.4 Proposed System

The proposed Notification Pro system introduces a comprehensive, premium-grade notification management solution that addresses all the shortcomings identified above:

  1. Automation — A persistent NotificationListenerService with foreground service and boot persistence ensures continuous, zero-interaction notification capture across all installed apps.
  2. Intelligence — Smart duplicate detection (composite key + time-based + ongoing notification tracking) and an extensible NotificationFilterRules engine with pattern matching for VLC, VPN, downloads, grouped messages, and ongoing calls remove noise automatically.
  3. Rich Data Extraction — App icons, profile avatars (EXTRA_LARGE_ICON), and shared images (EXTRA_PICTURE) are extracted, compressed, and stored alongside text content.
  4. Security — Multi-method app lock (PIN, pattern, biometric) with configurable timeout, lockout after failed attempts, and encrypted preferences protect sensitive data.
  5. Analytics & Organization — A Material Design 3 dashboard with daily/weekly/monthly statistics, interactive calendar, most-active-apps ranking, and per-app notification history gives users full visibility.
  6. Scalable Architecture — MVVM with Repository pattern, Room database with indexed queries, Paging 3 for large datasets, and WorkManager for background tasks ensure the app performs well at scale.

2.5 Comparative Analysis

FeatureNative Android LogBasic Third-Party AppsNotification Pro

3.5 Feasibility Study

3.5.1 Technical Feasibility

The project is technically feasible as it leverages well-established Android platform APIs and libraries. The core notification interception mechanism is built on Android's NotificationListenerService, which has been available since API 18 (Android 4.3) and is well-documented. The MVVM architecture is a recommended pattern by Google for Android development. All libraries used (Room, WorkManager, Paging 3, Biometric API) are part of the official Android Jetpack suite with long-term support guarantees.

The development tools — Android Studio, Kotlin, Gradle — are industry-standard and freely available. The developer possesses the necessary skills in Kotlin, Android development, and software architecture to complete the project within the given timeframe.

3.5.2 Economic Feasibility

The project has minimal economic requirements. All development tools (Android Studio, Kotlin, Android SDK) are free and open-source. No external APIs or paid services are required, as all data processing and storage occurs locally on the device. The project can be developed using a standard laptop/desktop computer and tested on either physical Android devices or free emulators. There are no recurring costs associated with servers, databases, or cloud services.

3.5.3 Operational Feasibility

The application is designed for ease of use with a modern, intuitive Material Design 3 interface. Users need to perform a one-time setup to grant Notification Listener Service permission, after which the application operates entirely automatically. The familiar Android navigation patterns (bottom navigation, swipe gestures, pull-to-refresh) ensure that users can adopt the application with minimal learning curve. The dark/light mode toggle and customizable settings further enhance usability for diverse user preferences.

3.5.4 Schedule Feasibility

The project follows an Agile development methodology with iterative sprints, allowing for incremental feature development and testing. The development timeline is structured as follows:

PhaseDurationActivities
Phase 1: PlanningWeek 1–2Requirements analysis, architecture design, UI wireframing
Phase 2: Core DevelopmentWeek 3–6Notification capture service, Room database, basic UI
Phase 3: FeaturesWeek 7–10Search, filtering, security module, analytics dashboard
Phase 4: PolishWeek 11–12UI refinement, dark mode, performance optimization
Phase 5: TestingWeek 13–14Multi-device testing, bug fixes, documentation

Chapter 4: System Design & Architecture

4.1 Development Methodology

The project follows the Agile Development Methodology with iterative sprints. The development process was organized into five phases: Requirements Analysis, System Design, Implementation, Testing, and Optimization. Each phase produced deliverables that were reviewed and refined before proceeding to the next iteration.

4.2 Architectural Pattern — MVVM

The application follows the MVVM (Model-View-ViewModel) architectural pattern with a clean Repository layer that abstracts data access. ViewModels use Kotlin Coroutines and StateFlow/Flow for reactive data binding, ensuring the UI automatically updates when new notifications arrive. Manual dependency injection keeps the app lightweight without requiring frameworks like Dagger or Hilt.

Figure 4.1: MVVM Architecture Diagram

UI Layer (Activities / Fragments / XML Layouts)

⬇ ⬆

ViewModel Layer (StateFlow / Coroutines)

⬇ ⬆

Repository Layer (NotificationRepository / AppRepository)

⬇ ⬆

Data Layer (Room Database / DAOs / Entities)


NotificationCaptureService
(Foreground Service)
CleanupWorker
(WorkManager)
AppLockManager
(Security Module)
Figure 4.1: MVVM Architecture with Service and Worker components

4.3 Project Structure

Figure 4.2: Project Structure Tree

app/src/main/java/com/notificationpro/app/
├── NotificationProApp.kt          ← Application class (initialization)
├── data/                          ← Data Layer
│   ├── AppDatabase.kt             ← Room database configuration
│   ├── Converters.kt              ← Type converters for Room
│   ├── dao/                       ← Data Access Objects
│   │   ├── NotificationDao.kt     ← Notification CRUD operations
│   │   └── AppDao.kt              ← App management operations
│   ├── entity/                    ← Database Entities
│   │   ├── NotificationEntity.kt  ← Notification data model
│   │   └── AppEntity.kt           ← App settings data model
│   └── repository/                ← Repository Layer
│       ├── NotificationRepository.kt
│       └── AppRepository.kt
├── service/                       ← Background Services
│   ├── NotificationCaptureService.kt  ← Core capture service
│   └── NotificationFilterRules.kt     ← Smart noise filtering
├── security/                      ← Security Module
│   ├── AppLockManager.kt          ← Runtime lock state management
│   └── AppLockPreferences.kt      ← Encrypted security preferences
├── receiver/                      ← Broadcast Receivers
│   └── BootReceiver.kt            ← Auto-start on boot
├── ui/                            ← UI Layer (MVVM)
│   ├── MainActivity.kt            ← Main entry point
│   ├── dashboard/                 ← Dashboard & analytics
│   ├── notifications/             ← Notification list & search
│   ├── saved/                     ← Saved/bookmarked notifications
│   ├── detail/                    ← Notification detail view
│   ├── apps/                      ← App management screens
│   └── security/                  ← Lock screen & security settings
├── util/                          ← Utility Classes
│   ├── PermissionUtils.kt
│   └── TimeUtils.kt
└── worker/                        ← Background Workers
    └── CleanupWorker.kt           ← Periodic notification cleanup

4.4 Use Case Diagram

Figure 4.3: Use Case Diagram

Use Case Diagram — Notification Pro

Actor: Android User

Use Case IDUse Case NameDescription
UC-01Grant Listener PermissionUser grants Notification Listener access via Android Settings
UC-02View DashboardUser views analytics (today/week/month counts, top apps, calendar)
UC-03Browse NotificationsUser scrolls through captured notification history
UC-04Search NotificationsUser performs full-text search by title, text, or app name
UC-05Save/Bookmark NotificationUser stars important notifications to protect from auto-delete
UC-06View Notification DetailUser taps notification to see full content, media, and metadata
UC-07Export as PDFUser exports notification data as PDF document
UC-08Share NotificationUser shares notification content via Android share intent
UC-09Manage Per-App SettingsUser enables/disables capture for specific apps
UC-10Configure Auto-DeleteUser sets 24-hour auto-delete for selected apps
UC-11Setup App LockUser configures PIN, pattern, or biometric authentication
UC-12AuthenticateUser unlocks app using chosen authentication method
UC-13Toggle Dark/Light ModeUser switches between visual themes
UC-14Delete NotificationsUser deletes individual or batch notifications

System Actor: Android OS

Use Case IDUse Case NameDescription
UC-S1Post NotificationOS delivers StatusBarNotification to NotificationCaptureService
UC-S2Boot CompletedOS broadcasts BOOT_COMPLETED to BootReceiver for service restart
UC-S3Trigger CleanupWorkManager executes daily CleanupWorker for old notifications
Figure 4.3: Use Case Diagram showing User and System interactions

4.5 Database Design

4.5.1 Entity-Relationship Diagram

The application uses two primary entities stored in an SQLite database managed by Android Room. The notifications table stores captured notification data, and the apps table manages per-app settings and statistics. The relationship is one-to-many: one app can have many notifications, linked via the packageName field.

Figure 4.4: Database Schema

Table 4.1: notifications

ColumnTypeConstraintsDescription
idLONGPRIMARY KEY, AUTOUnique notification ID
packageNameSTRINGINDEXEDPackage name of source app
appNameSTRING—Display name of source app
titleSTRING—Notification title
textSTRING—Notification body text
bigTextSTRING?NULLABLEExpanded text content
timestampLONGINDEXEDCapture time (epoch ms)
categorySTRING?NULLABLEAndroid notification category
notificationKeySTRING?NULLABLESystem notification key
isSavedBOOLEANINDEXED, DEFAULT falseBookmark/save status
appIconBLOB?NULLABLECompressed app icon (PNG, 96×96)
largeIconBLOB?NULLABLEProfile avatar (JPEG, max 200px)
pictureBLOB?NULLABLEShared image (JPEG, max 400px)

Table 4.2: apps

ColumnTypeConstraintsDescription
packageNameSTRINGPRIMARY KEYUnique package identifier
appNameSTRING—Display name
isEnabledBOOLEANDEFAULT trueCapture enabled/disabled
autoDeleteEnabledBOOLEANDEFAULT false24-hour auto-delete flag
notificationCountINTDEFAULT 0Total notifications captured
lastNotificationTimeLONG?NULLABLEMost recent notification time
appIconBLOB?NULLABLEApp icon (compressed PNG)

→ StatusBarNotification →

DFD Level 1 — Process Decomposition

ProcessInputOutputData Store
P1: Capture NotificationStatusBarNotification from OSNotificationEntityD1: notifications table
P2: Filter & DeduplicateRaw notification dataFiltered notification or SKIPIn-memory cache
P3: Extract MediaNotification extras (Bundle)Compressed byte arraysD1: notifications table
P4: Display & SearchUser query / browse requestPaginated notification listD1: notifications table
P5: Manage AppsUser toggle / settingsUpdated app settingsD2: apps table
P6: Authenticate UserPIN / Pattern / BiometricUnlock / DenyD3: Encrypted preferences
P7: Cleanup Old DataTimer trigger (daily)Deleted old recordsD1: notifications table
Figure 4.6: DFD Level 1 — Detailed process decomposition

4.7 Sequence Diagram — Notification Capture Flow

Figure 4.7: Sequence Diagram

Android OS          CaptureService         FilterRules          Repository           Room DB
    |                    |                     |                    |                   |
    |--postNotification->|                     |                    |                   |
    |                    |--shouldFilter()----->|                    |                   |
    |                    |<---false (pass)------|                    |                   |
    |                    |                     |                    |                   |
    |                    |--isDuplicate()------>|                    |                   |
    |                    |<---false (unique)----|                    |                   |
    |                    |                     |                    |                   |
    |                    |--extractMedia()      |                    |                   |
    |                    |  (icons, images)     |                    |                   |
    |                    |                     |                    |                   |
    |                    |--insertNotification()-------------------->|                   |
    |                    |                     |                    |--countDuplicates-->|
    |                    |                     |                    |<--0 (no dups)------|
    |                    |                     |                    |--insert()--------->|
    |                    |                     |                    |<--rowId------------|
    |                    |<---rowId (success)--------------------------|                   |
    |                    |                     |                    |                   |
    |                    |--updateAppEntry()------------------------>|                   |
    |                    |                     |                    |--upsert()--------->|
    |                    |                     |                    |                   |
Figure 4.7: Sequence Diagram — Notification capture, filter, and storage flow

4.8 Activity Diagram — App Lock Authentication Flow

Figure 4.8: Activity Diagram

                    [App Launch / Resume]
                           |
                    {Is Lock Enabled?}
                     /            \
                   NO              YES
                   |                |
             [Show Main UI]   {Has Timeout Expired?}
                               /            \
                             NO              YES
                             |                |
                       [Show Main UI]   {Check Lock Method}
                                        /      |       \
                                      PIN   PATTERN  BIOMETRIC
                                       |      |         |
                                  [Show PIN] [Show    [Show
                                   Screen]  Pattern]  Biometric]
                                       \      |       /
                                        {Verify Credential}
                                        /              \
                                    SUCCESS           FAILURE
                                      |                  |
                              [Record Unlock]    [Increment Attempts]
                              [Show Main UI]           |
                                              {Attempts >= 5?}
                                               /          \
                                             NO            YES
                                             |              |
                                        [Show Error]   [Lockout 30s]
                                        [Retry]        [Show Timer]
