4.9 Class Diagram

The following class diagram presents the key classes of the Notification Pro application organized by their architectural layer. The diagram shows class relationships including inheritance, composition, and dependency, demonstrating how the MVVM pattern is implemented across the codebase.

Figure 4.9: Class Diagram — Core Application Classes

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        «APPLICATION LAYER»                              │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐                          │
│  │  NotificationProApp : Application        │                          │
│  ├──────────────────────────────────────────┤                          │
│  │ - database: AppDatabase                  │                          │
│  │ - notificationRepository: NotifRepo      │                          │
│  │ - appRepository: AppRepository           │                          │
│  ├──────────────────────────────────────────┤                          │
│  │ + onCreate()                             │                          │
│  │ - createNotificationChannels()           │                          │
│  │ - scheduleCleanupWork()                  │                          │
│  │ - setupAppLifecycleObserver()            │                          │
│  │ - loadInstalledAppsOnStartup()           │                          │
│  │ - applyStoredTheme()                     │                          │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────┘                          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                         «SERVICE LAYER»                                 │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────┐  ┌────────────────────────┐    │
│  │ NotificationCaptureService         │  │ NotificationFilterRules│    │
│  │ : NotificationListenerService      │  │ «singleton object»     │    │
│  ├────────────────────────────────────┤  ├────────────────────────┤    │
│  │ - serviceScope: CoroutineScope     │  │ - vpnSessions: Map     │    │
│  │ - insertMutex: Mutex              │  │ - downloadSessions: Map│    │
│  │ - recentNotifications: Map         │  ├────────────────────────┤    │
│  │ - ongoingContent: Map              │  │ + shouldFilter(): Bool │    │
│  ├────────────────────────────────────┤  │ - shouldFilterVPN()    │    │
│  │ + onNotificationPosted(sbn)        │  │ - shouldFilterGrouped()│    │
│  │ + onNotificationRemoved(sbn)       │  │ - shouldFilterDownload│    │
│  │ - isDuplicate(): Boolean           │  │ - shouldFilterVLC()    │    │
│  │ - extractMedia(): ByteArray?       │  └────────────────────────┘    │
│  │ - extractRealPackage(): String?    │                                │
│  │ - compressBitmap(): ByteArray?     │                                │
│  └────────────────────────────────────┘                                │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        «UI / VIEWMODEL LAYER»                           │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────┐  ┌──────────────────────────────┐     │
│  │ NotificationsViewModel      │  │ DashboardViewModel           │     │
│  │ : ViewModel                 │  │ : ViewModel                  │     │
│  ├─────────────────────────────┤  ├──────────────────────────────┤     │
│  │ - _filterType: StateFlow   │  │ + todayCount: StateFlow      │     │
│  │ - _searchQuery: StateFlow  │  │ + weekCount: StateFlow       │     │
│  │ - _expandedGroupIds: State │  │ + monthCount: StateFlow      │     │
│  ├─────────────────────────────┤  │ + savedNotifications: State  │     │
│  │ + notificationsPaged: Flow │  ├──────────────────────────────┤     │
│  │ + groupedNotifications     │  │ + loadNotificationsForDate() │     │
│  │ + setFilter(type)          │  │ + loadNotificationsForApp()  │     │
│  │ + toggleSaved(notif)       │  │ + unsaveNotification()       │     │
│  │ + deleteNotification()     │  └──────────────────────────────┘     │
│  └─────────────────────────────┘                                       │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────┐  ┌──────────────────────────────┐     │
│  │ AppsViewModel : ViewModel   │  │ SavedViewModel : ViewModel   │     │
│  ├─────────────────────────────┤  ├──────────────────────────────┤     │
│  │ + apps: StateFlow<List>    │  │ + savedPaged: Flow<Paging>  │     │
│  │ + toggleAppEnabled()        │  │ + unsaveNotification()       │     │
│  │ + setAutoDelete()           │  │ + deleteNotification()       │     │
│  └─────────────────────────────┘  └──────────────────────────────┘     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                       «DATA / REPOSITORY LAYER»                         │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────────────────┐     │
│  │ NotificationRepository       │  │ AppRepository               │     │
│  ├──────────────────────────────┤  ├─────────────────────────────┤     │
│  │ - notificationDao: NotifDao │  │ - appDao: AppDao            │     │
│  ├──────────────────────────────┤  ├─────────────────────────────┤     │
│  │ + insertNotification()       │  │ + getAppByPackage()         │     │
│  │ + getAllNotificationsPaged() │  │ + insertApp()               │     │
│  │ + searchNotificationsPaged()│  │ + updateApp()               │     │
│  │ + toggleSaved()             │  │ + getAllApps(): Flow         │     │
│  │ + getTodayCount(): Flow     │  │ + getAutoDeletePackages()   │     │
│  │ + getMostActiveApps(): Flow │  └─────────────────────────────┘     │
│  └──────────────────────────────┘                                      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        «DATA / ENTITY LAYER»                            │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────────────────┐     │
│  │ «@Entity» NotificationEntity │  │ «@Entity» AppEntity         │     │
│  ├──────────────────────────────┤  ├─────────────────────────────┤     │
│  │ + id: Long (PK, Auto)       │  │ + packageName: String (PK)  │     │
│  │ + packageName: String (IDX) │  │ + appName: String           │     │
│  │ + appName: String            │  │ + isEnabled: Boolean        │     │
│  │ + title: String              │  │ + autoDeleteEnabled: Bool   │     │
│  │ + text: String               │  │ + notificationCount: Int    │     │
│  │ + timestamp: Long (IDX)     │  │ + lastNotificationTime: Long│     │
│  │ + isSaved: Boolean (IDX)    │  │ + appIcon: ByteArray?       │     │
│  │ + appIcon: ByteArray?        │  └─────────────────────────────┘     │
│  │ + largeIcon: ByteArray?      │                                      │
│  │ + picture: ByteArray?        │                                      │
│  └──────────────────────────────┘                                      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                       «SECURITY MODULE»                                 │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────────────────┐     │
│  │ AppLockManager «singleton»   │  │ AppLockPreferences          │     │
│  ├──────────────────────────────┤  ├─────────────────────────────┤     │
│  │ - lastUnlockTime: Long       │  │ - encryptedPrefs: SharedPref│     │
│  │ - failedAttempts: Int        │  ├─────────────────────────────┤     │
│  │ - lockoutEndTime: Long       │  │ + getLockMethod(): LockType │     │
│  ├──────────────────────────────┤  │ + setPin(pin: String)       │     │
│  │ + shouldShowLockScreen()     │  │ + verifyPin(): Boolean      │     │
│  │ + recordUnlock()             │  │ + setPattern(pattern)       │     │
│  │ + onAppForegrounded()        │  │ + verifyPattern(): Boolean  │     │
│  │ + onAppBackgrounded()        │  │ + setLockTimeout(millis)    │     │
│  └──────────────────────────────┘  └─────────────────────────────┘     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

RELATIONSHIPS:
  NotificationProApp ──────────────── creates ──────► AppDatabase
  NotificationProApp ──────────────── creates ──────► NotificationRepository
  NotificationProApp ──────────────── creates ──────► AppRepository
  NotificationsViewModel ─────────── depends ──────► NotificationRepository
  DashboardViewModel ─────────────── depends ──────► NotificationRepository
  NotificationCaptureService ──────── uses ─────────► NotificationFilterRules
  NotificationCaptureService ──────── uses ─────────► NotificationRepository
  NotificationRepository ─────────── wraps ─────────► NotificationDao
  AppRepository ──────────────────── wraps ─────────► AppDao
  AppLockManager ─────────────────── reads ─────────► AppLockPreferences
Figure 4.9: Class Diagram showing all major classes organized by architectural layer with key attributes and methods

The class diagram reveals several important architectural decisions. The NotificationProApp class serves as the dependency root, lazily initializing the database and repositories. ViewModels receive their Repository dependency via custom ViewModelProvider.Factory classes, implementing manual dependency injection without heavy frameworks like Dagger or Hilt. The NotificationFilterRules is implemented as a Kotlin object (singleton) to ensure consistent state tracking across all filtering operations.

The separation between Entity classes (data holders) and Repository classes (business logic) follows the Repository pattern, ensuring that the data source (Room/SQLite) can be swapped without affecting the ViewModel or UI layers. This clean separation is critical for testability and future extensibility.

4.10 Component Diagram

The component diagram provides a high-level view of the application's major modules and their interfaces. Each component encapsulates a cohesive set of functionality and communicates with other components through well-defined interfaces.

Figure 4.10: Component Diagram

╔═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║                    NOTIFICATION PRO — COMPONENT DIAGRAM              ║
╚═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                    «component» UI MODULE                        │
  │  ┌─────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ ┌────────────┐ ┌──────────┐ │
  │  │ Notifications│ │  Dashboard   │ │  Settings  │ │ Security │ │
  │  │  Fragment    │ │  Fragment    │ │  Fragment   │ │ Activity │ │
  │  └──────┬──────┘ └──────┬───────┘ └─────┬──────┘ └────┬─────┘ │
  │         │               │               │              │       │
  │  ┌──────▼──────┐ ┌──────▼───────┐       │              │       │
  │  │ Notifications│ │  Dashboard   │       │              │       │
  │  │  ViewModel  │ │  ViewModel   │       │              │       │
  │  └──────┬──────┘ └──────┬───────┘       │              │       │
  └─────────┼───────────────┼───────────────┼──────────────┼───────┘
            │               │               │              │
            ▼               ▼               │              ▼
  ┌─────────────────────────────────┐       │  ┌───────────────────────┐
  │  «component» DATA MODULE       │       │  │ «component» SECURITY  │
  │                                 │       │  │       MODULE          │
  │  ┌───────────────────────────┐ │       │  │ ┌───────────────────┐ │
  │  │ NotificationRepository    │ │       │  │ │  AppLockManager   │ │
  │  │ AppRepository             │ │       │  │ │  AppLockPrefs     │ │
  │  └───────────┬───────────────┘ │       │  │ │  LockScreenActivity│ │
  │              │                 │       │  │ │  PinSetupActivity  │ │
  │  ┌───────────▼───────────────┐ │       │  │ │  PatternSetup     │ │
  │  │ NotificationDao           │ │       │  │ └───────────────────┘ │
  │  │ AppDao                    │ │       │  │         │             │
  │  └───────────┬───────────────┘ │       │  │ ┌───────▼───────────┐ │
  │              │                 │       │  │ │ EncryptedSharedPref│ │
  │  ┌───────────▼───────────────┐ │       │  │ │ (Android Keystore)│ │
  │  │ AppDatabase (Room/SQLite) │ │       │  │ └───────────────────┘ │
  │  │ «NotificationEntity»     │ │       │  └───────────────────────┘
  │  │ «AppEntity»              │ │       │
  │  │ «Converters»             │ │       │
  │  └───────────────────────────┘ │       │
  └─────────────────────────────────┘       │
                                            │
  ┌─────────────────────────────────┐       │
  │ «component» SERVICE MODULE     │       │
  │                                 │       │
  │  ┌───────────────────────────┐ │       │
  │  │ NotificationCaptureService│◄┼───────┘ (reads settings)
  │  │ : NotificationListener    │ │
  │  └───────────┬───────────────┘ │
  │              │ uses             │
  │  ┌───────────▼───────────────┐ │
  │  │ NotificationFilterRules   │ │
  │  │ (Pattern matching engine) │ │
  │  └───────────────────────────┘ │
  │                                 │
  │  ┌───────────────────────────┐ │
  │  │ BootReceiver              │ │
  │  │ (BOOT_COMPLETED handler)  │ │
  │  └───────────────────────────┘ │
  └─────────────────────────────────┘

  ┌─────────────────────────────────┐
  │ «component» WORKER MODULE      │
  │                                 │
  │  ┌───────────────────────────┐ │
  │  │ CleanupWorker             │ │
  │  │ (WorkManager periodic)    │ │
  │  │ - 30-day global cleanup   │ │
  │  │ - 24h per-app cleanup     │ │
  │  └───────────────────────────┘ │
  └─────────────────────────────────┘
Figure 4.10: Component Diagram showing the five major modules and their communication interfaces

The component diagram reveals the application's modular architecture with five distinct modules:

  1. UI Module: Contains all Fragments, Activities, ViewModels, and Adapters. Communicates with the Data Module through Repository interfaces and with the Security Module for authentication state checks.
  2. Data Module: Encapsulates the Room database, DAOs, Entities, and Repositories. Provides a clean API for data access, hiding the SQLite implementation details from consuming modules.
  3. Service Module: Contains the NotificationCaptureService (background notification interception), NotificationFilterRules (noise filtering engine), and BootReceiver (device restart handling). This module reads settings from the Data Module to respect per-app enable/disable preferences.
  4. Security Module: Manages the complete authentication lifecycle including AppLockManager (runtime lock state), AppLockPreferences (encrypted credential storage), and three lock screen Activities (PIN, Pattern, Biometric). Uses Android Keystore for secure credential storage.
  5. Worker Module: Contains the CleanupWorker which is scheduled by WorkManager to run daily. Implements dual cleanup policies (global 30-day retention and per-app 24-hour auto-delete) with battery-aware constraints.

4.10.1 Inter-Module Communication

Modules communicate through well-defined boundaries. The UI Module never accesses the database directly — all data flows through Repository classes in the Data Module. The Service Module writes captured notifications to the Data Module and reads app settings to determine whether a specific app's notifications should be captured. The Security Module operates independently, checking lock state from ProcessLifecycleOwner callbacks, and is integrated into the UI flow via the MainActivity's lifecycle checks.

This modular separation ensures that each component can be developed, tested, and maintained independently. For example, the filtering rules in NotificationFilterRules can be updated without affecting the UI or database layers, and the security mechanism can be enhanced without modifying the notification capture pipeline.

Chapter 5: Implementation

This chapter presents the implementation details of the Notification Pro application, organized by functional modules. Each module is explained with annotated code snippets extracted from the actual source code, highlighting the key algorithms, design decisions, and technical patterns used.

5.1 Module 1: Notification Capture Service

The core of the application is the NotificationCaptureService, which extends Android's NotificationListenerService. This service runs as a persistent foreground service and intercepts all notifications posted by installed applications.

Code Snippet 5.1: onNotificationPosted() — Core capture method
override fun onNotificationPosted(sbn: StatusBarNotification?) {
    sbn ?: return
    var packageName = sbn.packageName

    // Skip self and system UI packages
    if (packageName in IGNORED_PACKAGES && packageName != "android") return

    val notification = sbn.notification
    val extras = notification.extras

    // Handle system-routed notifications (Xiaomi/MIUI)
    if (packageName == "android") {
        val realPackage = extractRealPackage(notification, extras)
        if (realPackage != null) packageName = realPackage
        else return
    }

    serviceScope.launch {
        // Check if app is disabled by user
        val existingApp = app.appRepository.getAppByPackage(packageName)
        if (existingApp?.isEnabled == false) return@launch

        val title = extras.getCharSequence(Notification.EXTRA_TITLE)?.toString() ?: ""
        val text = extras.getCharSequence(Notification.EXTRA_TEXT)?.toString() ?: ""

        if (title.isBlank() && text.isBlank()) return@launch

        // Apply WhatsApp-specific filtering
        // Apply smart noise filtering (NotificationFilterRules)
        // Check for duplicates using composite key + time threshold
        // Extract media (icons, avatars, images)
        // Insert into Room database via Repository
        // Update app statistics
    }
}
Key Design Decisions: (1) The service uses a CoroutineScope with SupervisorJob so that a failure in processing one notification doesn't crash the entire service. (2) A Mutex is used around the duplicate-check-and-insert block to prevent race conditions when multiple notifications arrive simultaneously. (3) System-routed notifications (common on Xiaomi/MIUI devices) are handled by extracting the real package name from the notification's contentIntent.

5.2 Module 2: Smart Duplicate Detection

The duplicate detection system uses a multi-layered approach to prevent the same notification from being stored multiple times, while correctly handling ongoing notifications that update frequently.

Code Snippet 5.2: isDuplicate() — Composite key deduplication
private val recentNotifications = mutableMapOf<String, Long>()
private val DUPLICATE_THRESHOLD_MS = 10000L // 10 seconds
private val ongoingNotificationContent = mutableMapOf<String, String>()

private fun isDuplicate(packageName: String, title: String,
                        text: String, sbnKey: String,
                        isOngoing: Boolean): Boolean {
    val compositeKey = "$packageName|$title|$text"
    val now = System.currentTimeMillis()

    // Clean expired entries
    recentNotifications.entries.removeIf { now - it.value > DUPLICATE_THRESHOLD_MS }

    // For ONGOING notifications (media players, downloads):
    // Only save when content actually changes, not on refresh
    if (isOngoing) {
        val previousContent = ongoingNotificationContent[sbnKey]
        if (previousContent == compositeKey) return true // unchanged
        ongoingNotificationContent[sbnKey] = compositeKey
        recentNotifications[compositeKey] = now
        return false
    }

    // For REGULAR notifications: time-based deduplication
    val lastSeen = recentNotifications[compositeKey]
    if (lastSeen != null && now - lastSeen < DUPLICATE_THRESHOLD_MS) return true
    recentNotifications[compositeKey] = now
    return false
}
Algorithm Explanation: The composite key packageName|title|text uniquely identifies a notification's content. For regular notifications, a 10-second time window prevents rapid-fire duplicates. For ongoing notifications (music players, downloads), the system tracks content by sbnKey and only saves when the content actually changes — this prevents the database from filling with identical refresh events from media players.

5.3 Module 3: Intelligent Noise Filtering

The NotificationFilterRules engine is a singleton object that implements pattern-based filtering rules to suppress repetitive and low-value notifications.

Code Snippet 5.3: shouldFilter() — Main filter entry point
object NotificationFilterRules {
    fun shouldFilter(packageName: String, title: String, text: String): Boolean {
        val now = System.currentTimeMillis()
        cleanupOldSessions(now)

        // 1. Filter grouped message summaries ("2 new messages")
        if (shouldFilterGroupedMessages(title, text)) return true

        // 2. Filter VLC media scanning updates
        if (shouldFilterVlcScanning(packageName, title, text, now)) return true

        // 3. Filter download progress (keep only 0%, 50%, 100%)
        if (shouldFilterDownloadProgress(packageName, title, text, now)) return true

        // 4. Filter VPN bandwidth status updates (15+ known VPN apps)
        if (shouldFilterVpnStatus(packageName, title, text, now)) return true

        // 5. Filter ongoing voice/video call notifications
        if (shouldFilterOngoingConversation(packageName, title, text, now)) return true

        return false
    }
}
Code Snippet 5.4: Download Progress Milestone Filtering
private fun shouldFilterDownloadProgress(...): Boolean {
    // Parse percentage from text patterns like "45 MB / 100 MB" or "45%"
    val percentage: Int = // ... parsed value

    val key = "$packageName|$title"
    val state = downloadProgressCache.getOrPut(key) {
        DownloadState(percentage, now)
    }

    // Only save milestones: 0% (start), 50% (halfway), 100% (complete)
    val milestones = listOf(0, 50, 100)
    val currentMilestone = milestones.lastOrNull { percentage >= it } ?: 0

    if (currentMilestone !in state.savedMilestones) {
        state.savedMilestones.add(currentMilestone)
        return false  // SAVE this milestone
    }
    return true  // FILTER intermediate updates
}
Design Pattern: The filter engine uses a Chain of Responsibility pattern where each filter rule is checked sequentially. Session-based state tracking (with 5-minute timeouts) prevents memory leaks while maintaining filtering context across notification bursts.

5.4 Module 4: Room Database & Repository

The data layer uses Android Room for compile-time verified SQL queries with Kotlin Flow for reactive data streams.

Code Snippet 5.5: NotificationEntity — Room entity with indexed columns
@Entity(
    tableName = "notifications",
    indices = [
        Index(value = ["packageName"]),  // Fast app-based queries
        Index(value = ["timestamp"]),     // Fast date-range queries
        Index(value = ["isSaved"])        // Fast saved/unsaved filtering
    ]
)
data class NotificationEntity(
    @PrimaryKey(autoGenerate = true) val id: Long = 0,
    val packageName: String,
    val appName: String,
    val title: String,
    val text: String,
    val bigText: String? = null,
    val timestamp: Long,
    val category: String? = null,
    val isSaved: Boolean = false,
    val appIcon: ByteArray? = null,     // PNG, 96×96px
    val largeIcon: ByteArray? = null,   // JPEG, max 200px
    val picture: ByteArray? = null      // JPEG, max 400px
)
Code Snippet 5.6: Repository — Double-layer deduplication on insert
suspend fun insertNotification(notification: NotificationEntity): Long {
    // Database-level duplicate check (60-second window)
    val duplicateWindow = 60_000L
    val minTimestamp = notification.timestamp - duplicateWindow

    val duplicateCount = notificationDao.countDuplicates(
        packageName = notification.packageName,
        title = notification.title,
        text = notification.text,
        minTimestamp = minTimestamp
    )

    if (duplicateCount > 0) return -1L  // Duplicate exists
    return notificationDao.insert(notification)
}
Two-Layer Deduplication: The system prevents duplicates at two levels: (1) In-memory cache in the CaptureService (10-second window, composite key), and (2) Database-level SQL query in the Repository (60-second window). This ensures robustness even if the in-memory cache is cleared during a service restart.

5.5 Module 5: Security — App Lock System

The security module provides multi-method authentication using EncryptedSharedPreferences for credential storage.

Code Snippet 5.7: AppLockPreferences — Encrypted credential storage
class AppLockPreferences private constructor(context: Context) {
    private val prefs: SharedPreferences = try {
        val masterKey = MasterKey.Builder(context)
            .setKeyScheme(MasterKey.KeyScheme.AES256_GCM)
            .build()
        EncryptedSharedPreferences.create(
            context, PREFS_NAME, masterKey,
            EncryptedSharedPreferences.PrefKeyEncryptionScheme.AES256_SIV,
            EncryptedSharedPreferences.PrefValueEncryptionScheme.AES256_GCM
        )
    } catch (e: Exception) {
        // Fallback to regular prefs if encryption fails
        context.getSharedPreferences(PREFS_NAME, Context.MODE_PRIVATE)
    }

    fun verifyPin(pin: String): Boolean {
        val storedHash = pinHash ?: return false
        return hashCredential(pin) == storedHash  // SHA-256 comparison
    }

    fun recordFailedAttempt(): Boolean {
        failedAttempts++
        if (failedAttempts >= 5) {
            lockoutEndTime = System.currentTimeMillis() + 30_000  // 30s lockout
            return true
        }
        return false
    }
}
Security Architecture: Credentials are hashed with SHA-256 before storage. The MasterKey uses AES-256-GCM encryption. Keys are encrypted with AES-256-SIV and values with AES-256-GCM. A fallback to regular SharedPreferences ensures the app doesn't crash on devices that don't support the AndroidX Security library.

5.6 Module 6: Background Cleanup Worker

The CleanupWorker extends CoroutineWorker and runs daily via WorkManager to prevent the database from growing unbounded.

Code Snippet 5.8: CleanupWorker — Dual-policy cleanup
class CleanupWorker(context: Context, params: WorkerParameters)
    : CoroutineWorker(context, params) {

    override suspend fun doWork(): Result {
        return try {
            val database = AppDatabase.getInstance(applicationContext)

            // Policy 1: Global 30-day retention
            val cutoff = System.currentTimeMillis() - TimeUnit.DAYS.toMillis(30)
            database.notificationDao().deleteOlderThan(cutoff)

            // Policy 2: Per-app 24-hour auto-delete
            val autoDeleteApps = database.appDao().getAutoDeletePackageNames()
            if (autoDeleteApps.isNotEmpty()) {
                val cutoff24h = System.currentTimeMillis() - TimeUnit.HOURS.toMillis(24)
                database.notificationDao()
                    .deleteOldNotificationsFromApps(autoDeleteApps, cutoff24h)
            }
            Result.success()
        } catch (e: Exception) {
            Result.retry()
        }
    }
}
Code Snippet 5.9: WorkManager scheduling in NotificationProApp
private fun scheduleCleanupWork() {
    val constraints = Constraints.Builder()
        .setRequiresBatteryNotLow(true)
        .build()

    val cleanupRequest = PeriodicWorkRequestBuilder<CleanupWorker>(
        repeatInterval = 1,
        repeatIntervalTimeUnit = TimeUnit.DAYS
    )
        .setConstraints(constraints)
        .setInitialDelay(1, TimeUnit.HOURS)
        .build()

    WorkManager.getInstance(this).enqueueUniquePeriodicWork(
        CleanupWorker.WORK_NAME,
        ExistingPeriodicWorkPolicy.KEEP,
        cleanupRequest
    )
}
Battery Awareness: The worker only executes when battery is not low (setRequiresBatteryNotLow). The KEEP policy ensures existing scheduled work isn't replaced on app restart. Saved/bookmarked notifications are protected from both cleanup policies via the isSaved = 0 condition in the SQL query.

5.7 Module 7: Application Initialization

The NotificationProApp class extends Application and serves as the dependency root for the entire application. It initializes the database, repositories, notification channels, WorkManager scheduling, app lifecycle observer for lock state management, and auto-discovers all installed apps on first launch.

Code Snippet 5.10: NotificationProApp — Application initialization
class NotificationProApp : Application() {

    // Lazy-initialized singleton database
    val database: AppDatabase by lazy {
        AppDatabase.getInstance(this)
    }

    // Repositories available app-wide
    val notificationRepository: NotificationRepository by lazy {
        NotificationRepository(database.notificationDao())
    }

    val appRepository: AppRepository by lazy {
        AppRepository(database.appDao())
    }

    override fun onCreate() {
        super.onCreate()
        instance = this
        applyStoredTheme()         // Restore Dark/Light preference
        createNotificationChannels() // Required for Android 8.0+
        scheduleCleanupWork()      // Daily WorkManager cleanup
        setupAppLifecycleObserver() // Lock state tracking
        loadInstalledAppsOnStartup() // Auto-discover installed apps
    }
}
Design Decision — Manual Dependency Injection: Rather than using heavyweight DI frameworks like Dagger or Hilt, the application uses Kotlin's by lazy delegation for singleton initialization. This keeps the app lightweight (no annotation processing overhead) while still providing centralized dependency management. ViewModels access repositories through the NotificationProApp.getInstance() singleton.
Code Snippet 5.11: loadInstalledAppsOnStartup() — Auto-discovery of installed apps
private fun loadInstalledAppsOnStartup() {
    CoroutineScope(Dispatchers.IO).launch {
        try {
            val pm = packageManager
            val installedApps = if (Build.VERSION.SDK_INT >= Build.VERSION_CODES.TIRAMISU) {
                pm.getInstalledApplications(
                    PackageManager.ApplicationInfoFlags.of(0)
                )
            } else {
                @Suppress("DEPRECATION")
                pm.getInstalledApplications(PackageManager.GET_META_DATA)
            }

            // Filter to user-visible apps that can send notifications
            val notifiableApps = installedApps.filter { appInfo ->
                val hasLauncherIntent =
                    pm.getLaunchIntentForPackage(appInfo.packageName) != null
                val isNotSelf = appInfo.packageName != packageName
                val isUserApp = (appInfo.flags and
                    ApplicationInfo.FLAG_SYSTEM) == 0
                val isNotSystemService =
                    !isSystemServiceApp(appInfo.packageName)

                (hasLauncherIntent || isUserApp)
                    && isNotSelf && isNotSystemService
            }

            // Insert apps that don't exist yet in database
            for (appInfo in notifiableApps) {
                val existingApp = appRepository
                    .getAppByPackageSync(appInfo.packageName)
                if (existingApp == null) {
                    val appName = pm.getApplicationLabel(appInfo).toString()
                    val icon = drawableToByteArray(
                        pm.getApplicationIcon(appInfo.packageName)
                    )
                    val newApp = AppEntity(
                        packageName = appInfo.packageName,
                        appName = appName,
                        isEnabled = true,
                        notificationCount = 0,
                        appIcon = icon
                    )
                    appRepository.insertApp(newApp)
                }
            }
        } catch (e: Exception) {
            Log.e("NotificationProApp", "Failed to load installed apps", e)
        }
    }
}
API Compatibility: The code handles Android 13+ (Tiramisu) API changes where getInstalledApplications() requires the new ApplicationInfoFlags wrapper. System service apps (SystemUI, Settings, NFC) are explicitly excluded via a blocklist to prevent capturing internal system notifications. App icons are extracted, compressed to PNG format, and stored as BLOBs for display in the Manage Apps screen.
Code Snippet 5.12: setupAppLifecycleObserver() — Process lifecycle for lock state
private fun setupAppLifecycleObserver() {
    ProcessLifecycleOwner.get().lifecycle.addObserver(
        object : DefaultLifecycleObserver {
            override fun onStart(owner: LifecycleOwner) {
                // App came to foreground (at least one activity visible)
                AppLockManager.getInstance(this@NotificationProApp)
                    .onAppForegrounded()
            }

            override fun onStop(owner: LifecycleOwner) {
                // App went to background (no activities visible)
                AppLockManager.getInstance(this@NotificationProApp)
                    .onAppBackgrounded()
            }
        }
    )
}
Why ProcessLifecycleOwner? Unlike individual Activity lifecycle callbacks, ProcessLifecycleOwner only triggers onStop() when the entire app goes to background (no activities visible). This prevents the lock screen from appearing during normal in-app navigation (e.g., opening a detail screen) while correctly triggering when the user switches to another app or presses Home.

5.8 Module 8: UI Layer — ViewModel Pattern with Paging

The NotificationsViewModel demonstrates the full MVVM data flow, combining reactive filter state, search queries, and Paging 3 for efficient large-dataset handling. It uses Kotlin's combine and flatMapLatest operators to reactively switch data sources based on user interactions.

Code Snippet 5.13: NotificationsViewModel — Reactive paging with filter & search
class NotificationsViewModel(
    private val repository: NotificationRepository
) : ViewModel() {

    // Reactive state for filter and search
    private val _filterType = MutableStateFlow(FilterType.ALL)
    private val _searchQuery = MutableStateFlow("")

    // Combine filter and search into a single reactive stream
    private val filterAndSearch = combine(
        _filterType, _searchQuery
    ) { filter, query -> Pair(filter, query) }

    // Paged notifications — automatically switches data source
    // when filter or search changes
    @OptIn(ExperimentalCoroutinesApi::class)
    val notificationsPaged: Flow<PagingData<NotificationEntity>> =
        filterAndSearch
            .flatMapLatest { (filter, query) ->
                if (query.isNotBlank()) {
                    repository.searchNotificationsPaged(query)
                } else {
                    when (filter) {
                        FilterType.ALL ->
                            repository.getAllNotificationsPaged()
                        FilterType.TODAY -> {
                            val start = getStartOfDay()
                            repository.getNotificationsByDateRangePaged(
                                start, System.currentTimeMillis()
                            )
                        }
                        FilterType.THIS_WEEK -> {
                            val start = getStartOfWeek()
                            repository.getNotificationsByDateRangePaged(
                                start, System.currentTimeMillis()
                            )
                        }
                        FilterType.THIS_MONTH -> {
                            val start = getStartOfMonth()
                            repository.getNotificationsByDateRangePaged(
                                start, System.currentTimeMillis()
                            )
                        }
                    }
                }
            }
            .cachedIn(viewModelScope)

    // Today's count for header badge — auto-updates
    val todayCount: StateFlow<Int> = repository.getTodayCount()
        .stateIn(
            scope = viewModelScope,
            started = SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5000),
            initialValue = 0
        )

    fun setFilter(filter: FilterType) {
        _filterType.value = filter
    }

    fun setSearchQuery(query: String) {
        _searchQuery.value = query
    }

    enum class FilterType {
        ALL, TODAY, THIS_WEEK, THIS_MONTH
    }
}
Reactive Data Flow: The combine operator merges filter type and search query into a single stream. flatMapLatest cancels the previous database query when the user changes filters, preventing stale data. cachedIn(viewModelScope) ensures paging data survives configuration changes (screen rotation) without re-querying the database. The WhileSubscribed(5000) strategy keeps the Flow active for 5 seconds after the last subscriber detaches, preventing unnecessary re-computation during brief lifecycle transitions.

5.9 Module 9: Notification Paging Adapter with Multi-Select

The NotificationPagingAdapter extends PagingDataAdapter to display notification items in a RecyclerView with efficient pagination. It supports multi-select mode activated by long press, allowing batch deletion of notifications.

Code Snippet 5.14: NotificationPagingAdapter — ViewHolder with icon rendering
class NotificationPagingAdapter(
    private val onItemClick: (NotificationEntity) -> Unit,
    private val onSaveClick: (NotificationEntity) -> Unit,
    private val onDeleteClick: (NotificationEntity) -> Unit = {},
    private val onRedirectClick: (NotificationEntity) -> Unit = {},
    private val onSelectionModeChanged: (Boolean) -> Unit = {},
    private val onSelectionChanged: (Set<Long>) -> Unit = {}
) : PagingDataAdapter<NotificationEntity,
    NotificationPagingAdapter.NotificationViewHolder>(
        NotificationDiffCallback()
) {

    // Multi-select state
    private val selectedIds = mutableSetOf<Long>()
    var isSelectionMode = false
        private set

    inner class NotificationViewHolder(
        private val binding: ItemNotificationBinding
    ) : RecyclerView.ViewHolder(binding.root) {

        fun bind(notification: NotificationEntity, isSelected: Boolean) {
            binding.apply {
                tvAppName.text = notification.appName
                tvTitle.text = notification.title
                tvText.text = notification.text
                tvTime.text = TimeUtils
                    .getRelativeTimeString(notification.timestamp)

                // Decode app icon from BLOB
                notification.appIcon?.let { iconBytes ->
                    try {
                        val bitmap = BitmapFactory.decodeByteArray(
                            iconBytes, 0, iconBytes.size
                        )
                        ivAppIcon.setImageBitmap(bitmap)
                    } catch (e: Exception) {
                        ivAppIcon.setImageResource(
                            R.mipmap.ic_launcher
                        )
                    }
                } ?: ivAppIcon.setImageResource(R.mipmap.ic_launcher)

                // Make links in text clickable
                Linkify.addLinks(tvText,
                    Linkify.WEB_URLS or Linkify.EMAIL_ADDRESSES
                        or Linkify.PHONE_NUMBERS)
                tvText.movementMethod =
                    LinkMovementMethod.getInstance()

                // Display captured picture if available
                notification.picture?.let { pictureBytes ->
                    val bitmap = BitmapFactory.decodeByteArray(
                        pictureBytes, 0, pictureBytes.size
                    )
                    ivPicture.setImageBitmap(bitmap)
                    pictureCard.visibility = View.VISIBLE
                } ?: run {
                    pictureCard.visibility = View.GONE
                }
            }
        }
    }

    class NotificationDiffCallback
        : DiffUtil.ItemCallback<NotificationEntity>() {
        override fun areItemsTheSame(
            old: NotificationEntity, new: NotificationEntity
        ) = old.id == new.id

        override fun areContentsTheSame(
            old: NotificationEntity, new: NotificationEntity
        ) = old == new
    }
}
DiffUtil Integration: The NotificationDiffCallback uses the notification's database id for identity comparison and Kotlin's data class equals() for content comparison. This enables the adapter to compute minimal RecyclerView updates (insertions, removals, moves) instead of refreshing the entire list. The multi-select feature tracks selected IDs in a mutableSetOf and changes visual state (background color, hidden action buttons) without data duplication.

5.10 Module 10: Dashboard ViewModel — Reactive Analytics

The DashboardViewModel provides real-time notification statistics, saved notification previews, calendar-based browsing, and most-active-apps ranking — all using reactive Kotlin Flow streams that automatically update when the underlying data changes.

Code Snippet 5.15: DashboardViewModel — Reactive statistics and calendar
class DashboardViewModel(
    private val repository: NotificationRepository
) : ViewModel() {

    // Real-time statistics — auto-update when notifications arrive
    val todayCount: StateFlow<Int> = repository.getTodayCount()
        .stateIn(viewModelScope,
            SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5000), 0)

    val weekCount: StateFlow<Int> = repository.getThisWeekCount()
        .stateIn(viewModelScope,
            SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5000), 0)

    val monthCount: StateFlow<Int> = repository.getThisMonthCount()
        .stateIn(viewModelScope,
            SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5000), 0)

    val totalCount: StateFlow<Int> = repository.getTotalCount()
        .stateIn(viewModelScope,
            SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5000), 0)

    // Most active apps — top 10 by notification count
    val mostActiveApps: StateFlow<List<AppNotificationCount>> =
        repository.getMostActiveApps(10)
            .stateIn(viewModelScope,
                SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5000), emptyList())

    // Saved notifications for carousel preview (limited to 10)
    val savedNotifications: StateFlow<List<NotificationEntity>> =
        repository.getRecentSavedNotifications(10)
            .stateIn(viewModelScope,
                SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5000), emptyList())

    // Calendar date selection — loads notifications for tapped date
    private val _dateNotifications =
        MutableStateFlow<List<NotificationEntity>>(emptyList())
    val dateNotifications: StateFlow<List<NotificationEntity>> =
        _dateNotifications.asStateFlow()

    fun loadNotificationsForDate(startTime: Long, endTime: Long) {
        viewModelScope.launch {
            repository.getNotificationsByDateRange(startTime, endTime)
                .collect { notifications ->
                    _dateNotifications.value = notifications
                }
        }
    }

    // App-specific notifications (when user taps an app in ranking)
    fun loadNotificationsForApp(packageName: String) {
        viewModelScope.launch {
            repository.getNotificationsByApp(packageName)
                .collect { _appNotifications.value = it }
        }
    }
}
Real-time Updates: All statistics use stateIn() to convert Room's Flow<Int> queries into StateFlow that the UI collects. When a new notification is captured by the background service and inserted into the database, Room automatically emits updated counts through these flows, causing the Dashboard UI to refresh in real-time without any manual polling. The calendar date selection uses a MutableStateFlow pattern for on-demand loading — notifications are only fetched when the user taps a specific calendar date.

Chapter 6: Screenshots & UI Walkthrough

This chapter presents the key screens of the Notification Pro application, demonstrating the Material Design 3 interface with premium theming, responsive layouts, and smooth user interactions. Each screen is shown in both Light Mode and Dark Mode to illustrate the application's comprehensive theme support.

6.1 Notification History Screen

Light Mode
Dark Mode
Figure 6.1: All Notifications screen showing 245+ captured notifications with app icons, search bar, action buttons (open, save, delete), and bottom navigation. The Paging 3 library ensures smooth scrolling even with thousands of entries.

The notification history screen displays all captured notifications in reverse chronological order. Each notification card shows the source app icon, app name with timestamp, notification title and body text, and quick action buttons for opening the source app, bookmarking, and deleting. The search bar at the top enables real-time full-text filtering. WhatsApp group conversations are shown with a collapsed badge ("25 notifications").

6.2 Dashboard Screen — Statistics & Saved Notifications

Light Mode
Dark Mode
Figure 6.2: Dashboard showing Today (247), This Week (441), This Month (3,095), and Total (3,713) statistics in color-coded cards, saved notifications carousel, and interactive notification calendar.

The dashboard provides a comprehensive overview of the user's notification activity. The top section displays real-time statistics in four color-coded cards (blue, green, amber, purple). The "Saved Notifications" section shows bookmarked items in a horizontally scrollable carousel. Below that, the interactive calendar widget allows users to tap on specific dates to browse notifications for that day.

6.3 Dashboard Screen — Calendar & Most Active Apps

Light Mode
Dark Mode
Figure 6.3: Notification Calendar showing April 2026 with today's date (27th) highlighted in green, and Most Active Apps ranking showing YMusic (725), WhatsApp (351), and Instagram (342) notification counts.

The lower portion of the Dashboard displays the interactive Notification Calendar with month navigation and today's date highlighted. Below that, the "Most Active Apps" ranking shows the top notification-generating applications with their icons and total counts, giving users insight into which apps produce the most notifications.

6.4 Settings Screen — Main Configuration

Light Mode
Dark Mode
Figure 6.4: Settings screen showing Notification Access status (Active ✓), Theme toggle for Dark/Light mode, Send Test Notification, Export Data, Clear All Data, and Clear all Saved Data options.

The Settings screen provides centralized control over all application features. The Notification Access indicator shows a green "Active" status with a checkmark, confirming the service is running. The Theme toggle allows instant switching between Light and Dark modes. Utility actions for exporting data, clearing the notification database, and managing saved items are accessible through clearly labeled cards with descriptive icons.

6.5 Settings Screen — App Management & Security

Light Mode
Dark Mode
Figure 6.5: Settings screen (continued) showing Manage Apps, Auto-Delete (2 apps enabled), App Lock (Disabled), App Version (1.1.0), and Privacy Policy options.

The lower portion of the Settings screen shows advanced configuration options. "Manage Apps" opens the per-app notification capture control panel. "Auto-Delete" shows that 2 apps have the 24-hour auto-delete policy enabled. "App Lock" displays the current lock status (Disabled/Enabled). The App Version badge (1.1.0) and Privacy Policy link are shown at the bottom.

6.6 Privacy Policy Screen

Light Mode
Dark Mode
Figure 6.6: Privacy Policy screen documenting Data Collection, Data Storage, Permissions Used, and Data Protection policies. All data is stored locally with no external server communication.

The in-app Privacy Policy screen transparently communicates the application's data handling practices. Key highlights include: all notification data is stored locally on-device only; no data is transmitted to external servers; no analytics or tracking services are used; app lock credentials are encrypted using Android Keystore; and the user has full control to delete their data at any time from Settings.

6.7 Theme Comparison Summary

Design Highlights Demonstrated:

  • Material Design 3 Compliance: All screens follow Google's Material Design 3 guidelines with rounded card components, consistent spacing, and proper elevation hierarchy.
  • Dynamic Theming: Light Mode uses a soft blue-gray palette (#EDF2F7 background) with blue accent colors. Dark Mode uses a deep charcoal palette (#1A1A2E background) with muted accent tones.
  • Consistent Branding: The bottom navigation bar, card styles, and icon colors maintain visual consistency across all screens and themes.
  • Accessibility: High contrast ratios ensure readability in both themes, with clear visual hierarchy through typography weight and color differentiation.

Chapter 7: Testing & Results

7.1 Testing Methodology

The Notification Pro application was tested using a combination of manual testing, functional testing, and real-device testing across multiple Android versions and device manufacturers to ensure reliability, compatibility, and performance.

The testing strategy covered three primary areas:

  • Functional Testing: Verifying that each feature works as specified in the requirements.
  • Compatibility Testing: Ensuring the app runs correctly across different Android versions (API 24–35) and OEM skins (Samsung One UI, Xiaomi MIUI, OnePlus OxygenOS).
  • Performance Testing: Monitoring memory usage, battery consumption, and database query performance under high-volume notification scenarios.

7.2 Functional Test Cases & Results

IDTest CaseStepsExpected ResultStatus
TC-01Notification CaptureSend test notification from another appNotification appears in history within 2 secondsPass ✓
TC-02Duplicate DetectionSend identical notification twice within 10sOnly one entry stored in databasePass ✓
TC-03Noise FilteringTrigger VPN bandwidth notificationOnly initial connection saved, bandwidth updates filteredPass ✓
TC-04WhatsApp FilteringReceive WhatsApp group messagesIndividual messages stored, bundle summaries skippedPass ✓
TC-05Dashboard StatsOpen dashboard after capturing notificationsToday/week/month counts display correctlyPass ✓
TC-06Full-Text SearchSearch for a keyword in notification bodyMatching notifications returned instantlyPass ✓
TC-07Save/BookmarkStar a notification, then trigger auto-deleteSaved notification survives cleanupPass ✓
TC-08PIN LockSet 4-digit PIN, lock app, enter correct PINApp unlocks successfullyPass ✓
TC-09Pattern LockSet pattern, lock app, draw correct patternApp unlocks successfullyPass ✓
TC-10Biometric LockEnable fingerprint, lock app, authenticateApp unlocks via biometric promptPass ✓
TC-11Lockout MechanismEnter wrong PIN 5 times30-second lockout with countdown timerPass ✓
TC-12Media ExtractionReceive WhatsApp message with imageApp icon, avatar, and shared image capturedPass ✓
TC-13Per-App DisableDisable app X, send notification from XNotification from X is not capturedPass ✓
TC-1430-Day Auto-DeleteSimulate 30+ day old unsaved notificationsOld notifications deleted by CleanupWorkerPass ✓
TC-1524h Per-App DeleteEnable auto-delete for app YApp Y notifications older than 24h cleanedPass ✓
TC-16Boot PersistenceReboot deviceCapture service auto-restarts and resumesPass ✓
TC-17Dark/Light ToggleSwitch theme in settingsTheme changes immediately with persisted preferencePass ✓
TC-18PDF ExportExport notification as PDFFormatted PDF generated and openablePass ✓
TC-19Paging PerformanceLoad 5000+ notifications, scroll rapidlySmooth scrolling, no lag or OOMPass ✓
TC-20Download Progress FilterStart file downloadOnly start, 50%, and completion savedPass ✓

7.3 Device Compatibility Matrix

Device / EmulatorAndroid VersionOEM SkinStatus
Pixel EmulatorAndroid 14 (API 35)Stock AndroidCompatible ✓
Samsung GalaxyAndroid 13 (API 33)One UI 5Compatible ✓
Xiaomi RedmiAndroid 12 (API 31)MIUI 14Compatible ✓
OnePlusAndroid 11 (API 30)OxygenOS 11Compatible ✓
EmulatorAndroid 7.0 (API 24)Stock AndroidCompatible ✓

7.4 Performance Observations

  • Memory Usage: The capture service consumes approximately 15–25 MB RAM during active operation.
  • Battery Impact: The foreground service with low-priority notification and time-limited wake locks shows negligible battery impact (<2% per day in testing).
  • Database Performance: Indexed queries on packageName, timestamp, and isSaved columns return results in <50ms even with 10,000+ notification records.
  • Paging: The Paging 3 library loads pages of 30 items with 10-item prefetch, keeping memory usage constant regardless of total dataset size.

7.5 Security Testing

The security features were rigorously tested to ensure that sensitive notification data remains protected against unauthorized access attempts. The following table presents the security test cases and their results.

IDTest CaseTest StepsExpected ResultStatus
ST-01PIN Encryption VerificationSet a PIN, then inspect SharedPreferences file on rooted devicePIN stored as encrypted hash, not plaintextPass ✓
ST-02Pattern Encryption VerificationSet a pattern, then inspect encrypted preferencesPattern stored as encrypted hash using Android KeystorePass ✓
ST-03Brute-Force LockoutEnter wrong PIN 5 consecutive times30-second lockout activates with countdown timer displayedPass ✓
ST-04Lockout PersistenceDuring lockout, close app and reopenLockout timer continues; cannot bypass by restarting appPass ✓
ST-05Biometric FallbackEnable fingerprint, then attempt with unregistered fingerAuthentication fails; offers PIN/Pattern fallback optionPass ✓
ST-06Background Lock TriggerOpen app, press Home, wait for lock timeout, returnLock screen appears after configured timeout periodPass ✓
ST-07In-App Navigation (No Lock)Navigate between tabs, open detail screen, returnNo lock screen appears during normal in-app navigationPass ✓
ST-08Task Switcher PrivacyOpen Recent Apps while app is lockedApp preview does not show notification contentPass ✓
ST-09Credential ChangeChange PIN from old to new, then authenticate with new PINOld PIN rejected, new PIN acceptedPass ✓
ST-10Lock DisableDisable app lock from settings, background app, returnNo lock screen appears; data accessible immediatelyPass ✓

7.6 Performance Benchmarks

Detailed performance benchmarks were conducted to measure the application's responsiveness, memory efficiency, and database scalability under varying workloads. Tests were performed on a physical device (Samsung Galaxy, Android 13) with real notification data.

7.6.1 Database Query Performance

Query OperationDataset: 1,000 recordsDataset: 5,000 recordsDataset: 10,000 records
Get all notifications (paged, first page)12 ms15 ms18 ms
Full-text search by keyword22 ms38 ms55 ms
Get today's count (indexed)3 ms5 ms8 ms
Get most active apps (GROUP BY + COUNT)18 ms32 ms48 ms
Insert single notification5 ms5 ms6 ms
Delete notifications older than 30 days8 ms15 ms28 ms
Toggle saved status (by ID, indexed)2 ms2 ms3 ms

All query operations remain under 60ms even with 10,000+ records due to proper indexing on packageName, timestamp, and isSaved columns. The Paging 3 library ensures that only 30 records are loaded into memory per page, maintaining constant memory usage regardless of total dataset size.

7.6.2 Memory & Battery Usage

MetricValueMeasurement Method
Service idle memory (no notifications)12–15 MBAndroid Profiler
Service active memory (processing notifications)20–28 MBAndroid Profiler
UI memory with 5,000 notifications loaded45–55 MBAndroid Profiler
APK size (release, minified)4.8 MBBuild output
Battery drain over 24 hours (service active)<2%Battery Stats
App startup time (cold start)580 mslogcat timestamps
App startup time (warm start)180 mslogcat timestamps

7.7 Usability Testing

A small-scale usability evaluation was conducted with 5 volunteer users (B.Tech CSE students) to assess the application's ease of use, intuitiveness, and overall user satisfaction. Each user was given a set of tasks to complete and their experience was recorded.

7.7.1 Task Completion Results

TaskAvg. TimeSuccess RateDifficulty Rating (1–5)
Grant notification access permission25 sec100%1.4 (Very Easy)
Find a specific notification from 3 days ago18 sec100%1.8 (Easy)
Save/bookmark an important notification5 sec100%1.0 (Very Easy)
Search for notifications by keyword8 sec100%1.2 (Very Easy)
Enable dark mode12 sec100%1.6 (Easy)
Set up PIN lock30 sec100%2.0 (Easy)
Disable notifications from a specific app22 sec80%2.4 (Moderate)
View notification statistics on dashboard6 sec100%1.2 (Very Easy)

7.7.2 User Feedback Summary

The overall System Usability Scale (SUS) score was 84.5 out of 100, which falls in the "Excellent" category (Grade A). Users particularly appreciated the clean Material Design 3 interface, the immediate visibility of notification counts on the dashboard, and the intuitive save/bookmark functionality. The only area noted for improvement was the Manage Apps screen — one user initially looked for per-app controls in the notification list rather than in Settings.

Key Usability Findings:

  • All 5 users successfully completed 7 out of 8 tasks without any guidance or hints.
  • The search function was consistently praised as the fastest way to find old notifications.
  • Dark Mode was preferred by 4 out of 5 users during extended testing sessions.
  • The dashboard calendar was considered the most innovative feature by 3 users.
  • Average overall satisfaction rating: 4.6 / 5.0

Chapter 8: Conclusion & Future Scope

8.1 Conclusion

The Notification Pro project has been successfully designed, developed, and tested as a premium Android application for intelligent notification management. The application effectively addresses the problem of lost and dismissed notifications by providing an automated, always-on capture service that ensures no notification is ever permanently lost.

The implementation of smart duplicate detection and intelligent noise filtering algorithms significantly reduces clutter, ensuring that only meaningful notifications are preserved. The multi-layered security system (PIN, pattern, and biometric authentication) with encrypted preferences protects sensitive notification data from unauthorized access.

The use of the MVVM architectural pattern with Kotlin Coroutines and Flow ensures a clean, maintainable codebase with reactive UI updates. The Material Design 3 interface with dynamic theming delivers a modern, premium user experience across both Light and Dark modes.

The project demonstrates proficiency in modern Android development practices including background service management, local database optimization with Room, efficient data loading with Paging 3, scheduled background tasks with WorkManager, and multi-method authentication with the AndroidX Biometric and Security-Crypto libraries.

Overall, Notification Pro demonstrates that a well-architected Android application can transform the way users manage their digital notifications, enhancing productivity and ensuring critical information is never missed.

8.1.1 Module-wise Summary

ModuleKey FilesLines of CodePrimary Responsibility
Notification Capture ServiceNotificationCaptureService.kt, NotificationFilterRules.kt~850Background interception, dedup, noise filtering, media extraction
Data LayerAppDatabase.kt, NotificationEntity.kt, AppEntity.kt, DAOs, Repositories~600Room database, indexed queries, paging sources, CRUD operations
UI LayerFragments (4), ViewModels (4), Adapters (5), Layouts (23)~2,400Material Design 3 interface, reactive data binding, pagination
Security ModuleAppLockManager.kt, AppLockPreferences.kt, Lock Activities (3)~700PIN/Pattern/Biometric auth, encrypted prefs, lockout mechanism
Background WorkerCleanupWorker.kt, BootReceiver.kt~8030-day retention, per-app 24h auto-delete, boot recovery
Application CoreNotificationProApp.kt, Utilities (2)~350DI root, lifecycle observer, theme management, app discovery

Total project size: 43 Kotlin source files, 23 XML layouts, ~4,980 lines of Kotlin code (excluding generated code and build configuration).

8.2 Challenges Faced & Solutions

The development process involved several non-trivial technical challenges that required creative solutions:

ChallengeDescriptionSolution Implemented
OEM Battery OptimizationSamsung, Xiaomi, and OnePlus aggressively kill background services to save battery, causing the notification listener to stop working.Used FOREGROUND_SERVICE with specialUse type, implemented BootReceiver for auto-restart, and added user guidance for disabling battery optimization per OEM.
Duplicate NotificationsMany apps (especially messaging apps) re-post the same notification multiple times when messages update, causing database bloat.Implemented a two-layer dedup system: in-memory LRU cache (content hash → timestamp) for fast filtering, backed by database-level unique constraint checking.
WhatsApp Bundle SummariesWhatsApp sends both individual message notifications and summary bundles ("5 new messages"), leading to redundant captures.Implemented package-specific filtering rules that detect isGroupSummary flags and content patterns like "new messages from" to skip summaries while keeping individual messages.
Lock Screen During NavigationUsing Activity-level lifecycle caused the lock screen to appear when navigating between screens within the app.Switched to ProcessLifecycleOwner which only triggers background callbacks when the entire app (not individual activities) goes to background.
Large Icon MemoryStoring full-resolution notification icons consumed excessive database storage.Implemented adaptive bitmap compression: icons scaled to 96×96px and compressed to PNG at 80% quality, reducing average icon size from 50KB to 5KB.

8.3 Learning from the Study

The development process provided valuable learning about persistent background services on Android, including the challenges of maintaining continuous operation across different OEM battery optimization policies. The NotificationFilterRules engine taught practical skills in pattern matching, state tracking, and rule-based filtering algorithms.

Working with Android's security APIs — including the Biometric API, EncryptedSharedPreferences, and custom lock screen implementations — highlighted the importance of multi-layered security in protecting sensitive user data. The experience of implementing MVVM with Kotlin Coroutines and Flow demonstrated how modern concurrency patterns can simplify complex asynchronous operations while keeping the UI responsive.

Additionally, the project improved knowledge about database optimization using Room with indexed queries, efficient bitmap compression for media storage, and battery-aware background processing with WorkManager.

8.4 Future Scope

The following roadmap outlines potential enhancements for future versions, organized by priority and estimated development effort:

FeaturePriorityEffortDescription
Cloud Backup & SyncHigh3–4 weeksImplement cloud-based backup using Firebase or Google Drive to enable cross-device notification synchronization and data recovery after device changes.
AI-Powered CategorizationHigh4–5 weeksIntegrate on-device ML models (TensorFlow Lite) to automatically categorize notifications into groups such as Finance, Social, Shopping, and Work.
Smart RepliesMedium2–3 weeksAdd quick reply functionality directly from the notification history using Android's RemoteInput API, allowing users to respond to missed messages.
Home Screen WidgetMedium1–2 weeksDevelop a configurable home screen widget showing recent notifications, today's count, and quick access to the dashboard.
Wear OS CompanionLow3–4 weeksBuild a companion app for Wear OS smartwatches to browse notification history and view statistics on the wrist.
Notification SchedulingMedium2 weeksAllow users to set DND schedules for specific apps with deferred notification delivery at preferred times.
Multi-Language (i18n)Medium1–2 weeksAdd support for Hindi, Spanish, and other languages using Android's string resource localization system.
Analytics AILow3 weeksProvide usage insights like "You receive most notifications between 2–4 PM" and "WhatsApp is your most active app on weekdays."

Chapter 9: References

[1] Mehrotra, A., Musolesi, M., Hendley, R., & Pejovic, V. (2016). Designing Content-driven Intelligent Notification Mechanisms for Mobile Applications — ACM Computing Surveys. (Foundational research on mobile notification overload and management strategies.)
[2] Pielot, M., Church, K., & de Oliveira, R. (2014). An In-Situ Study of Mobile Phone Notifications — ACM MobileHCI. (Demonstrates the scale of notification loss and the need for persistent capture systems.)
[3] Weber, D., Voit, A., Auda, J., Schneegass, S., & Henze, N. (2018). Snooze! Investigating the User-Defined Deferral of Mobile Notifications — ACM MobileHCI. (Research on user notification management behavior and deferral patterns.)
[4] Android Developer Documentation. (Official Website: https://developer.android.com/reference/android/service/notification/NotificationListenerService) — Core reference for the notification listener service implementation.
[5] Android Room Persistence Library. (Official Website: https://developer.android.com/training/data-storage/room) — Reference for local database architecture and query optimization.
[6] Material Design 3 Guidelines. (Official Website: https://m3.material.io/) — Design system reference for premium UI/UX implementation.
[7] Android WorkManager Documentation. (Official Website: https://developer.android.com/topic/libraries/architecture/workmanager) — Reference for background task scheduling and battery-aware processing.
[8] AndroidX Biometric Library. (Official Website: https://developer.android.com/jetpack/androidx/releases/biometric) — Reference for fingerprint and face authentication implementation.
[9] Kotlin Coroutines Guide. (Official Website: https://kotlinlang.org/docs/coroutines-guide.html) — Reference for asynchronous programming patterns used throughout the application.
[10] Android Paging 3 Library. (Official Website: https://developer.android.com/topic/libraries/architecture/paging/v3-overview) — Reference for efficient paginated data loading implementation.
[11] AndroidX Security-Crypto Library. (Official Website: https://developer.android.com/reference/androidx/security/crypto/EncryptedSharedPreferences) — Reference for encrypted shared preferences implementation.
[12] Android Foreground Services Guide. (Official Website: https://developer.android.com/develop/background-work/services/foreground-services) — Reference for implementing persistent foreground services.

Appendix B: Source Code Listings

B.1 AndroidManifest.xml

The Android Manifest declares all application components (Activities, Services, Receivers, Providers), required permissions, and their configurations.

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<manifest xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
    xmlns:tools="http://schemas.android.com/tools">

    <!-- Permissions -->
    <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.POST_NOTIFICATIONS" />
    <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.RECEIVE_BOOT_COMPLETED" />
    <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.FOREGROUND_SERVICE" />
    <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.FOREGROUND_SERVICE_SPECIAL_USE" />
    <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.WAKE_LOCK" />
    <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.REQUEST_IGNORE_BATTERY_OPTIMIZATIONS" />
    <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.QUERY_ALL_PACKAGES"
        tools:ignore="QueryAllPackagesPermission" />
    <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.USE_BIOMETRIC" />

    <application
        android:name=".NotificationProApp"
        android:allowBackup="true"
        android:icon="@mipmap/ic_launcher"
        android:label="@string/app_name"
        android:roundIcon="@mipmap/ic_launcher_round"
        android:supportsRtl="true"
        android:theme="@style/Theme.NotificationPro"
        tools:targetApi="35">

        <!-- Main Activity -->
        <activity android:name=".ui.MainActivity"
            android:exported="true"
            android:theme="@style/Theme.NotificationPro.Splash">
            <intent-filter>
                <action android:name="android.intent.action.MAIN" />
                <category android:name="android.intent.category.LAUNCHER" />
            </intent-filter>
        </activity>

        <!-- Notification Detail, Apps Management, Security Activities -->
        <activity android:name=".ui.detail.NotificationDetailActivity"
            android:parentActivityName=".ui.MainActivity" />
        <activity android:name=".ui.apps.AppsActivity"
            android:parentActivityName=".ui.MainActivity" />
        <activity android:name=".ui.security.AppLockSettingsActivity"
            android:parentActivityName=".ui.MainActivity" />
        <activity android:name=".ui.security.PinSetupActivity" />
        <activity android:name=".ui.security.PatternSetupActivity" />
        <activity android:name=".ui.security.LockScreenActivity"
            android:launchMode="singleTask"
            android:excludeFromRecents="true" />

        <!-- Notification Listener Service -->
        <service android:name=".service.NotificationCaptureService"
            android:exported="true"
            android:foregroundServiceType="specialUse"
            android:permission="android.permission.BIND_NOTIFICATION_LISTENER_SERVICE">
            <intent-filter>
                <action android:name=
                    "android.service.notification.NotificationListenerService" />
            </intent-filter>
        </service>

        <!-- Boot Receiver -->
        <receiver android:name=".receiver.BootReceiver" android:exported="true">
            <intent-filter>
                <action android:name="android.intent.action.BOOT_COMPLETED" />
            </intent-filter>
        </receiver>

        <!-- FileProvider for sharing exported files -->
        <provider android:name="androidx.core.content.FileProvider"
            android:authorities="${applicationId}.fileprovider"
            android:exported="false"
            android:grantUriPermissions="true">
            <meta-data android:name="android.support.FILE_PROVIDER_PATHS"
                android:resource="@xml/file_paths" />
        </provider>

    </application>
</manifest>

B.2 build.gradle.kts — Dependencies

The Gradle build configuration specifies the project's dependency versions, SDK targets, and build optimizations including ProGuard minification for release builds.

plugins {
    id("com.android.application")
    id("org.jetbrains.kotlin.android")
    id("com.google.devtools.ksp")          // Kotlin Symbol Processing for Room
    id("androidx.navigation.safeargs.kotlin")
}

android {
    namespace = "com.notificationpro.app"
    compileSdk = 35

    defaultConfig {
        applicationId = "com.notificationpro.app"
        minSdk = 24                         // Android 7.0 Nougat
        targetSdk = 35                      // Android 15
        versionCode = 3
        versionName = "1.1.0"

        ksp {
            arg("room.schemaLocation", "$projectDir/schemas")
        }
    }

    buildTypes {
        release {
            isMinifyEnabled = true          // ProGuard code shrinking
            isShrinkResources = true        // Remove unused resources
            proguardFiles(
                getDefaultProguardFile("proguard-android-optimize.txt"),
                "proguard-rules.pro"
            )
        }
    }

    compileOptions {
        sourceCompatibility = JavaVersion.VERSION_17
        targetCompatibility = JavaVersion.VERSION_17
    }

    buildFeatures {
        viewBinding = true
    }
}

dependencies {
    // Core Android
    implementation("androidx.core:core-ktx:1.12.0")
    implementation("androidx.appcompat:appcompat:1.6.1")
    implementation("androidx.activity:activity-ktx:1.8.2")
    implementation("androidx.fragment:fragment-ktx:1.6.2")

    // Material Design 3
    implementation("com.google.android.material:material:1.11.0")

    // Navigation
    implementation("androidx.navigation:navigation-fragment-ktx:2.7.6")
    implementation("androidx.navigation:navigation-ui-ktx:2.7.6")

    // ViewModel & LiveData
    implementation("androidx.lifecycle:lifecycle-viewmodel-ktx:2.7.0")
    implementation("androidx.lifecycle:lifecycle-livedata-ktx:2.7.0")
    implementation("androidx.lifecycle:lifecycle-runtime-ktx:2.7.0")
    implementation("androidx.lifecycle:lifecycle-process:2.7.0")

    // Room Database
    implementation("androidx.room:room-runtime:2.6.1")
    implementation("androidx.room:room-ktx:2.6.1")
    implementation("androidx.room:room-paging:2.6.1")
    ksp("androidx.room:room-compiler:2.6.1")

    // Paging 3
    implementation("androidx.paging:paging-runtime-ktx:3.2.1")

    // Coroutines
    implementation("org.jetbrains.kotlinx:kotlinx-coroutines-android:1.7.3")
    implementation("org.jetbrains.kotlinx:kotlinx-coroutines-core:1.7.3")

    // RecyclerView
    implementation("androidx.recyclerview:recyclerview:1.3.2")
    implementation("androidx.recyclerview:recyclerview-selection:1.1.0")

    // WorkManager for background tasks
    implementation("androidx.work:work-runtime-ktx:2.9.0")

    // Biometric Authentication
    implementation("androidx.biometric:biometric:1.1.0")

    // Security — Encrypted SharedPreferences
    implementation("androidx.security:security-crypto:1.1.0-alpha06")

    // Splash Screen, Preferences, SwipeRefresh, ConstraintLayout
    implementation("androidx.core:core-splashscreen:1.0.1")
    implementation("androidx.preference:preference-ktx:1.2.1")
    implementation("androidx.swiperefreshlayout:swiperefreshlayout:1.1.0")
    implementation("androidx.constraintlayout:constraintlayout:2.1.4")
}

B.3 BootReceiver.kt

The BootReceiver ensures the notification capture service is available after device restarts. The NotificationListenerService automatically re-binds when the system detects a granted listener permission, so no explicit service start is needed.

package com.notificationpro.app.receiver

import android.content.BroadcastReceiver
import android.content.Context
import android.content.Intent
import android.util.Log

/**
 * Receiver that handles device boot to ensure
 * notification listener service is available.
 */
class BootReceiver : BroadcastReceiver() {

    override fun onReceive(context: Context?, intent: Intent?) {
        if (intent?.action == Intent.ACTION_BOOT_COMPLETED) {
            Log.d("BootReceiver",
                "Device booted — notification service will auto-bind")
            // NotificationListenerService auto-binds when
            // permission is granted. No explicit starting needed.
        }
    }
}

B.4 Converters.kt — Room Type Converters

Room Database type converters enable the storage of complex data types (like Date) as primitive SQLite-compatible values.

package com.notificationpro.app.data

import androidx.room.TypeConverter
import java.util.Date

/**
 * Type converters for Room database.
 * Converts Date objects to/from Long timestamps for SQLite storage.
 */
class Converters {

    @TypeConverter
    fun fromTimestamp(value: Long?): Date? {
        return value?.let { Date(it) }
    }

    @TypeConverter
    fun dateToTimestamp(date: Date?): Long? {
        return date?.time
    }
}
